old_claim.note: Smartphones provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Mobile broadband is modeled as cellular-network infrastructure: GSMA tracks mobile internet connectivity through 3G, 4G, and 5G coverage and download-speed trends.

source_shape.source_locator: Explore the latest global data section: GSMA presents mobile internet connectivity through 3G, 4G, and 5G coverage plus download-speed trends.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: GSMA frames mobile broadband connectivity as cellular network coverage across 3G, 4G, and 5G generations.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports cellular-network generations as the upstream infrastructure lineage for mobile broadband, while smartphones are user devices and not required for the network category.

ontology_before: Mobile broadband networks depended on smartphones, conflating end-user devices with cellular network infrastructure.
ontology_after: Mobile broadband networks depend on cellular network infrastructure and standards lineage, while smartphones remain downstream/user equipment.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: mobile_broadband_networks now depends on cellular_networks and no longer directly depends on smartphones.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to smartphone mobile internet usage rather than network infrastructure.
A source showed the first mobile broadband networks required smartphones rather than cellular standards and network equipment.
A narrower smartphone_broadband_usage node is added and intentionally depends on smartphones.
short_reason: Mobile broadband is a cellular-network generation, not a smartphone-dependent technology.
